entries.title: How do I start mining SOST?
entries.short_answer: Follow the Mine SOST in 10 minutes guide on the public website: install build dependencies, clone sost-core, build, create a wallet, start the node, then start the miner pointing at your own SOST address.

entries.title: Why does SOST currently focus on solo mining?
entries.short_answer: SOST is experimenting with a solo-miner-first model during the trial to keep block discovery distributed and avoid early concentration in a few pool operators.

entries.title: Why does my miner find a block that the node rejects?
entries.short_answer: Most often it is a race-loss: another miner found a block first and your candidate now points at a stale parent. Less commonly it means your miner version is older than the consensus rules the node now enforces.

entries.title: What is an orphan block?
entries.short_answer: An orphan block is a valid block that lost the race to another block at the same height. The chain accepts only one of them; the other is discarded.

entries.title: Why are 64 threads slower than 16 threads?
entries.short_answer: ConvergenceX is memory-bandwidth bound. Past a certain thread count, threads compete for the same memory bus and the per-thread useful work drops faster than the thread count rises. The optimum is usually 16 or 32 on consumer hardware and rarely much higher even on servers.

entries.title: What is cASERT in plain words?
entries.short_answer: cASERT is the difficulty-adjustment system. It computes the next bitsQ from recent block timing and may switch the miner to an easier or harder profile when the chain is running ahead of or behind schedule.

entries.title: Are Useful Compute rewards active?
entries.short_answer: No. Useful Compute infrastructure is live for testing, but rewards are postponed.

entries.title: What is the SOST AI Engine?
entries.short_answer: SOST has an internal research and review engine. It does not publish autonomously and it does not decide consensus.

entries.title: Does GeaSpirit guarantee minerals at depth?
entries.short_answer: No. GeaSpirit produces probabilistic exploratory candidates only. Presence requires field validation.

entries.title: Are Materials Engine candidates validated?
entries.short_answer: Only when the candidate is explicitly backed by DFT/CHGNet output or known public data. Otherwise it is an internal hypothesis.

entries.title: Is the SOST DEX fully trustless?
entries.short_answer: No. The current DEX is a trust-minimised, operator-assisted alpha. Some flows still depend on the operator and that is documented in the public DEX page.

entries.title: Do I ever need to expose my secret credentials?
entries.short_answer: Never. No SOST tool, page or person will ever ask for them.

items.title: How do I start mining SOST?
items.question: How do I start mining SOST on a fresh Ubuntu machine?
items.short_answer: Follow the Mine SOST in 10 minutes guide on the public website: install build dependencies, clone sost-core, build, create a wallet, start the node, then start the miner pointing at your own SOST address.
items.detailed_answer: The public guide at /sost-mine.html walks through every step. The short version: install build-essential, cmake, git, libssl-dev and libsecp256k1-dev; clone the repo; run cmake .. -DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=Release and cmake --build . -j; create a wallet with ./sost-cli newwallet; start the node; and finally start the miner with --address sost1... and a thread count appropriate to your hardware. Try 16 or 32 threads first because ConvergenceX is memory-bandwidth bound; more threads is not always better.
warnings: Solo mining has high variance. You may go hours without finding a block.
There is no exchange listing. SOST is in pre-market testing.

items.title: Why does SOST currently focus on solo mining?
items.question: Why is there no official mining pool for SOST?
items.short_answer: SOST is experimenting with a solo-miner-first model during the trial to keep block discovery distributed and avoid early concentration in a few pool operators.
items.detailed_answer: Most PoW chains become pool-dominated within weeks of launch — two or three pools controlling 60-80% of block production is the historical norm. SOST's choice during pre-market testing is to not run an official pool and not ship pool-friendly tooling, so block discovery stays close to individual operators while the network is small enough that it still matters. This is a stance for the trial window, not a permanent rule.
warnings: Variance is the price of solo mining — expect long gaps with no blocks.

items.title: Why does my miner find a block that the node rejects?
items.question: What does 'node REJECTED block' mean and what should I do?
items.short_answer: Most often it is a race-loss: another miner found a block first and your candidate now points at a stale parent. Less commonly it means your miner version is older than the consensus rules the node now enforces.
items.detailed_answer: A rejected-block log line means the node accepted your submission for inspection but refused to add it to the chain. The miner re-pulls the new tip and continues automatically. If the rejection is repeated, update both node and miner from main and rebuild — a recent fork (timestamp MTP rules at block 6400, staged relief at block 6550) tightens what counts as a valid block, so an older miner can produce candidates the node will refuse.

items.title: What is an orphan block?
items.question: What does orphan / stale block mean?
items.short_answer: An orphan block is a valid block that lost the race to another block at the same height. The chain accepts only one of them; the other is discarded.
items.detailed_answer: When two miners find a block at the same height nearly simultaneously, the network temporarily sees two competing tips. The chain converges on whichever side accumulates the next block first; the losing block becomes 'orphan' or 'stale' and its reward is lost. This is a normal part of decentralised mining, not a bug.

items.title: Why are 64 threads slower than 16 threads?
items.question: More threads should mean more attempts per second — why does my hashrate go down?
items.short_answer: ConvergenceX is memory-bandwidth bound. Past a certain thread count, threads compete for the same memory bus and the per-thread useful work drops faster than the thread count rises. The optimum is usually 16 or 32 on consumer hardware and rarely much higher even on servers.
items.detailed_answer: Compare the effective stable/s figure (not the raw att/s) across runs with --threads 16 and --threads 32. The thread count that gives the highest sustained eff stable/s is the one to use. Going to 64 or more typically reduces it on every machine we have profiled, including 192-core servers.

items.title: What is cASERT in plain words?
items.question: What is cASERT / bitsQ and why does my miner switch profiles?
items.short_answer: cASERT is the difficulty-adjustment system. It computes the next bitsQ from recent block timing and may switch the miner to an easier or harder profile when the chain is running ahead of or behind schedule.
items.detailed_answer: Each block has a base profile (H10, H11, H12, H13) chosen from the current lag. When a block takes much longer than the 10-minute target, cASERT applies a relief cascade — from block 6550 onwards this is a staged drop of three profile levels every minute, floored at E7. The profile your miner uses must match the profile the node expects, which is why the miner restarts on a profile change.

items.title: Are Useful Compute rewards active?
items.question: Are SOST Useful Compute rewards active right now?
items.short_answer: No. Useful Compute infrastructure is live for testing, but rewards are postponed.
items.detailed_answer: The Useful Compute trial worker can be run today against the public worker endpoint, but no rewards are paid for current activity and block 7000 will not activate any rewarded compute window. The rewarded phase is postponed until the final task design is properly defined, tested and announced in advance.
warnings: Do not assume any future Useful Compute reward; activation will be announced explicitly.

items.title: What is the SOST AI Engine?
items.question: Does SOST have an AI? Does it make decisions?
items.short_answer: SOST has an internal research and review engine. It does not publish autonomously and it does not decide consensus.
items.detailed_answer: The SOST AI Engine is internal-only. It generates hypotheses, reviews public wording, runs deterministic validators against canonical claims and stores its output in review packs that a human inspects before anything reaches the public website. There is no live public chatbot. Consensus is decided by code and validators, not by external models.

items.title: Does GeaSpirit guarantee minerals at depth?
items.question: If GeaSpirit returns a candidate target, does that mean a mineral is there?
items.short_answer: No. GeaSpirit produces probabilistic exploratory candidates only. Presence requires field validation.
items.detailed_answer: GeaSpirit's public methodology screens candidate areas from open data using a documented scoring function. A high-score candidate is a hypothesis worth further field study — it is not a confirmation that a deposit exists or that a particular metal is present at any depth. Any claim stronger than 'candidate' falls outside the public methodology and the project does not make it.
warnings: No GeaSpirit output is a promise of mineral presence or quantity.

items.title: Are Materials Engine candidates validated?
items.question: If Materials Engine ranks a formula highly, can I trust it?
items.short_answer: Only when the candidate is explicitly backed by DFT/CHGNet output or known public data. Otherwise it is an internal hypothesis.
items.detailed_answer: Materials Engine produces ranked candidate lists by running deterministic feature scoring over public datasets. A candidate appearing on a list is a starting point for review, not a validated material. The public site only labels a candidate as backed when DFT, CHGNet or peer-reviewed data accompanies it.

items.title: Is the SOST DEX fully trustless?
items.question: Can I treat the SOST DEX as a fully trustless market?
items.short_answer: No. The current DEX is a trust-minimised, operator-assisted alpha. Some flows still depend on the operator and that is documented in the public DEX page.
items.detailed_answer: The DEX architecture moves toward self-custody and encrypted peer-to-peer settlement, but during the alpha phase the operator still mediates parts of the flow. Calling it 'fully trustless' would overstate the current state. Read the DEX page for the exact custody and settlement model and treat any position as experimental.
warnings: DEX positions are experimental. There is no guaranteed market or redemption.

items.title: Do I ever need to expose my secret credentials?
items.question: Does any SOST tool ever ask for my secret credentials (key material, recovery words, wallet password)?
items.short_answer: Never. No SOST tool, page or person will ever ask for them.
items.detailed_answer: Anything that asks for your secret credentials — your key material, your recovery words or your wallet password — is a phishing attempt. Mining only needs your public SOST address. The miner binary, the explorer, the help center and the troubleshooter never request a secret credential. Treat any such request (in chat, email, DM, fake support page) as malicious by default.
warnings: Never reveal your wallet credentials to anyone.
The official website will never ask you to type those values into a form.
